scenarios.title: Credential Dumping via LSASS
scenarios.description: Simulate an attacker dumping credentials from LSASS memory using Mimikatz and ProcDump. One of the most common techniques seen in MDR cases.

mitre_techniques.name: OS Credential Dumping: Security Account Manager

expected_detections.detection: LSASS memory access by non-system process
ProcDump or comsvcs.dll usage
NTLM hash extraction attempt
Mimikatz signature in process memory

universal: LSASS credential dumping is the #1 technique we see in MDR cases after initial access
Our analysts detect this in real-time — you can see the alert fire within seconds of execution
The attacker only needs to run this once to get every password hash on the system
financial_services: In financial environments, these hashes can be used to move laterally to trading systems or payment infrastructure
PCI-DSS requires detection and response to credential theft — this shows your MDR coverage
healthcare: Credential dumping is step 2 in most healthcare ransomware attacks — after initial access, before lateral movement to clinical systems
HIPAA breach notification is triggered the moment PHI systems are reachable with stolen credentials
manufacturing: In OT/ICS environments, stolen credentials can provide access to engineering workstations and SCADA systems
This technique was used in the Colonial Pipeline attack to move from IT to OT networks

se_runbook.setup: Ensure Windows victim VM is running with Sophos agent active. Verify CALDERA agent (sandcat) is deployed on victim.
steps: Show the clean MDR console — no alerts
Launch the simulation from this UI
Switch to MDR console and watch alerts fire in real time
Walk through the alert detail — process tree, memory access, hash extraction
Show analyst response workflow — isolation, investigation, containment
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Deliver industry talking points while alerts are loading — typically 10-15 seconds after execution

scenarios.title: Discovery & Enumeration Chain
scenarios.description: Simulate a threat actor performing post-compromise discovery using built-in Windows tools (LOLBins). Maps to what attackers do in the first 5 minutes after gaining access.

expected_detections.detection: Rapid sequential execution of discovery commands
LOLBin enumeration pattern (net, ipconfig, whoami in sequence)
Domain enumeration via nltest or net group
Unusual process spawning from Office or browser

universal: Every attacker runs these commands within minutes of gaining access — it's how they figure out where they are and what's valuable
Individually these look like normal admin activity. Our MDR detects the *pattern* — the sequence and timing is the tell
This stage is critical to catch — if you miss it, the attacker moves on to lateral movement and credential theft
financial_services: Attackers are specifically looking for systems connected to trading platforms, payment rails, or SWIFT infrastructure
Network discovery commands reveal the path to high-value targets — catching this early stops the kill chain
healthcare: Discovery commands help attackers identify EHR systems, medical imaging servers, and backup locations
Ransomware groups specifically target backups first — this discovery phase is where they find them
manufacturing: In manufacturing environments, attackers use discovery to find historian servers and HMI systems
Mapping the network from an IT foothold is the first step toward OT intrusion

se_runbook.setup: No special setup required. Works with any Windows endpoint. Good first demo — low risk, highly visible.
steps: Show the MDR console — explain what baseline looks like
Launch the simulation — this one runs fast (under 60 seconds)
Point out how each individual command looks benign
Show how MDR correlates them into a single high-confidence alert
Highlight the MITRE ATT&CK mapping in the alert detail
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Start industry talking points immediately after launch — alerts appear fast on this one

scenarios.title: Phishing → Payload → Persistence
scenarios.description: Full kill chain simulation: phishing document triggers payload execution, which establishes persistence via scheduled task. Multi-step CALDERA operation.

expected_detections.detection: Office application spawning child process
mshta or wscript execution from user context
Scheduled task created by non-admin process
PowerShell encoded command execution
Persistence mechanism established post-execution

universal: This is the most common initial access path we see — phishing is still #1
The scary part isn't the phishing — it's the persistence. The attacker survives reboots and patch cycles
Our MDR caught this at the execution stage — before the attacker had time to move laterally
financial_services: Business email compromise starts exactly like this — a 'vendor invoice' attachment that looks legitimate
Once persistence is established, attackers wait weeks before acting — this is how wire fraud setups work
healthcare: Healthcare is the #1 targeted sector for phishing — high-pressure clinical environments mean staff click links
Ransomware deployment typically happens 2-3 weeks after this persistence stage — MDR breaks the chain here
manufacturing: Spearphishing targeting supply chain contacts is a major vector in manufacturing attacks
A persistent foothold on an engineering workstation is a gateway to OT systems

se_runbook.setup: CALDERA must be running and sandcat agent deployed on victim. This scenario uses CALDERA for multi-step orchestration. Allow 3-5 minutes for full chain execution.
steps: Brief the prospect on the scenario — 'we're simulating a phishing attack from click to persistence'
Launch from this UI — CALDERA will orchestrate the full chain
Walk through each stage as it executes: document open → child process → scheduled task
Show MDR detecting at the execution stage — before persistence completes ideally
If MDR catches it post-persistence, show the remediation workflow — task removal, process kill, isolation
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: This scenario takes 3-5 minutes — use the full industry talking points during execution

scenarios.title: Pass the Hash Lateral Movement
scenarios.description: Simulate an attacker harvesting NTLM hashes from a compromised host and using them to authenticate to remote systems without the plaintext password. Demonstrates the direct continuation of SCN-001 — stolen hashes become the keys to lateral movement.

mitre_techniques.name: OS Credential Dumping: Security Account Manager
Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash

expected_detections.detection: SMB network share enumeration from non-admin process
NTLM authentication without interactive logon (Pass the Hash pattern)
Anomalous lateral movement to C$ admin share
Impacket toolkit signature in process telemetry

universal: Pass the Hash is why credential dumping is so dangerous — once the attacker has the hash, they never need the password
There's no failed login attempt, no password prompt — standard perimeter monitoring is completely blind to this
Sophos MDR detects the authentication anomaly: same hash used from multiple source IPs is an immediate red flag
financial_services: In financial environments, lateral movement via Pass the Hash is how attackers traverse from a workstation to treasury systems or payment servers
Attackers specifically target service accounts — one hash can unlock dozens of systems on the same domain
healthcare: EHR systems and clinical workstations often share service account credentials — Pass the Hash creates a direct path to patient data
HIPAA requires detecting unauthorized access to PHI systems — MDR catches this before the attacker reaches clinical data
manufacturing: OT/IT boundary systems often use shared domain accounts — a single hash can bridge the gap from corporate to plant floor
Lateral movement to historian servers or HMI systems is the critical step before OT disruption

se_runbook.setup: Run SCN-001 first or explain that hashes are already harvested. CALDERA agent must be deployed on Windows victim. Works best as the second scenario in a kill-chain narrative.
steps: Frame it: 'Now the attacker has hashes from SCN-001 — watch what they do next'
Launch the simulation — share enumeration fires first
Point out the authentication step: 'No password. Just the hash. The system can't tell the difference'
Show the MDR alert: anomalous NTLM authentication pattern
Explain: without MDR, this lateral movement is completely invisible to traditional tools
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Deliver the 'no password needed' line immediately when the PtH step executes — highest-impact moment

scenarios.title: PowerShell Execution & AMSI Bypass
scenarios.description: Simulate an attacker abusing PowerShell for obfuscated command execution and disabling the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI) to prevent script scanning. Demonstrates the most common technique attackers use to evade endpoint AV before pulling down stage-2 payloads.

mitre_techniques.name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

expected_detections.detection: PowerShell -EncodedCommand execution from non-interactive session
AMSI tamper attempt via memory patching (amsi.dll integrity)
PowerShell WebClient with suspicious user-agent string
IEX download cradle pattern in PowerShell ScriptBlock log

universal: PowerShell is the #1 tool attackers use post-compromise — it's built into every Windows endpoint and trusted by default
AMSI bypass is step zero before running any malicious script — attackers have to disable scanning before pulling down the payload
Sophos Endpoint integrates directly into AMSI — we see the script content even when it's obfuscated or encoded
financial_services: Living-off-the-land PowerShell attacks leave minimal disk artifacts — they're designed to evade forensic investigation
Encoded PowerShell commands are the delivery mechanism for most banking trojans and wire fraud malware
healthcare: Healthcare environments often whitelist PowerShell for legitimate IT admin use — attackers exploit that trust
Remote access tools deployed via PowerShell cradles are the typical first step in healthcare ransomware attacks
manufacturing: PowerShell is often unrestricted on manufacturing workstations — attackers use it to avoid raising alerts on engineering systems
ICS/OT reconnaissance scripts are frequently delivered via PowerShell download cradles from a compromised IT host

se_runbook.setup: No special setup required. Good general-purpose demo showing attacker evasion techniques. Highlights Sophos Endpoint AMSI integration specifically.
steps: Introduce the concept: 'The attacker is already in. Now they need to run their tools without triggering AV'
Launch simulation — encoded command fires first
Show the PowerShell ScriptBlock log telemetry in Sophos XDR
Explain AMSI bypass step: 'This is how they blind traditional AV before pulling down the real payload'
Show how Sophos Endpoint memory integrity monitoring catches the bypass attempt
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Pause on the AMSI bypass step — this is the key differentiator for Sophos Endpoint. Spend 30-60 seconds on the detection story here.

scenarios.title: Ransomware Pre-Deployment Simulation
scenarios.description: Simulate the four-phase ransomware pre-deployment sequence: disable backup services, narrate shadow copy deletion, stage a ransom note and encrypted file markers, then generate the Sophos XDR detection handoff. Designed to show how MDR interrupts a ransomware attack before any files are actually encrypted.

mitre_techniques.name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

expected_detections.detection: VSS and WBEngine service disabled by non-system process
vssadmin or wmic shadowcopy delete command execution
Ransom note file creation (README_RECOVER_FILES.txt pattern)
Mass file extension change to .encrypted
High-confidence ransomware precursor — automatic MDR escalation

universal: Ransomware operators spend weeks inside a network before they encrypt anything — they're maximizing blast radius
Shadow copy deletion is the single highest-confidence ransomware signal — when we see it, we escalate immediately
Sophos MDR stops ransomware at the pre-deployment phase — before a single file is encrypted
financial_services: The average ransomware demand in financial services is now $4.7M — and that's before regulatory fines and reputational damage
Wire transfer fraud and ransomware are increasingly combined — attackers encrypt and extort simultaneously
MDR's 24/7 coverage means we catch ransomware at 3am on a Sunday — when your team isn't watching
healthcare: Healthcare ransomware directly impacts patient care — hospitals have diverted ambulances and cancelled surgeries after attacks
The average healthcare ransomware attack costs $1.4M and takes 54 days to recover from — MDR prevention is orders of magnitude cheaper
HIPAA breach notification requirements mean every encrypted patient record is a reportable incident
manufacturing: Ransomware on OT systems stops production lines — the cost per hour of downtime often exceeds the ransom demand itself
Manufacturing is the #1 targeted sector for ransomware — attackers know operational disruption creates payment pressure
MDR catches the IT foothold before attackers ever reach OT systems

se_runbook.setup: This is the highest-impact demo. Save it for the close. CALDERA agent must be deployed on Windows victim. Allow 3-5 minutes for full chain. Have Sophos XDR console ready to switch to at the end.
steps: Frame it: 'This is the scenario your prospect fears most. Let's show them exactly how we stop it.'
Launch simulation — walk through each phase as it executes
Phase 1 (defense disable): 'This is the attacker preparing the battlefield — removing your recovery options'
Phase 2 (shadow copies): 'This is the point of no return in most ransomware attacks — we catch it here'
Phase 3 (ransom note): 'Show the actual ransom note file in File Explorer on the Windows VM — very visual'
Switch to Sophos XDR: show the critical alerts firing, analyst escalation workflow, isolation recommendation
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: This scenario is the conversation-changer. Take your time. Use the full vertical talking points during phases 2 and 3.

scenarios.title: NDR: Brute Force Simulation
scenarios.description: Simulates a brute force attack by rapidly attempting connections to 10 common authentication services (SSH, RDP, SMB, FTP, LDAP, MySQL, PostgreSQL, VNC, MSSQL, Telnet). Generates the connection pattern that NDR sensors flag as credential brute forcing.

expected_detections.detection: Multiple failed authentication attempts across services
Rapid connection attempts from single source to authentication ports

universal: Brute force attacks remain one of the most common initial access techniques. NDR detects the network pattern — rapid connection attempts to authentication ports — even if individual login attempts don't generate endpoint alerts.
Unlike endpoint detection which requires an agent on the target, NDR sees brute force attempts against any device on the network including IoT, printers, and unmanaged systems.
financial_services: Financial institutions face constant credential brute force attempts against customer portals, trading systems, and internal databases. NDR provides visibility across all network segments.
healthcare: Medical devices and legacy healthcare systems often lack endpoint agents. NDR detects brute force attempts against these unprotected devices.
manufacturing: OT/ICS systems like PLCs and HMIs are frequently targeted by brute force attacks. NDR monitors these segments without requiring agents on sensitive equipment.

se_runbook.setup: Ensure victim VM is on the same subnet as the target IP (default: 192.168.1.100). NDR sensor must be monitoring the network segment.
steps: Show the NDR dashboard with no active alerts
Launch SCN-010 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA execute brute force attempts (~30 seconds)
Switch to NDR console to show brute force detection alerts
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Deliver talking points while brute force attempts are running — the live CALDERA feed shows connection attempts in real time

scenarios.title: NDR: UDP Data Exfiltration
scenarios.description: Simulates data exfiltration by sending 10MB of UDP packets to a suspicious external IP address (203.0.113.100). Tests NDR detection of anomalous outbound data volume over UDP on a non-standard port.

mitre_techniques.name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Unencrypted/Obfuscated Non-C2 Protocol

expected_detections.detection: High-volume UDP data transfer to unusual external IP
Anomalous outbound data volume on non-standard port

universal: Data exfiltration over UDP is harder to detect than TCP because UDP is connectionless — there's no handshake to monitor. NDR uses traffic volume analysis and behavioral baselines to catch bulk UDP transfers.
Attackers increasingly use UDP for exfiltration because many firewalls and proxies focus on TCP/HTTP inspection. NDR provides visibility into all protocol types.
financial_services: Financial data exfiltration carries severe regulatory consequences (PCI DSS, SOX). NDR detects bulk data transfers before they leave the network perimeter.
healthcare: HIPAA requires monitoring for unauthorized data transfers. NDR detects PHI exfiltration attempts across all protocols including UDP.
manufacturing: Intellectual property theft via network exfiltration is a top concern. NDR monitors for unusual data volumes leaving the OT network.

se_runbook.setup: NDR sensor must be monitoring the network segment. The target IP (203.0.113.100) is a safe RFC 5737 documentation address — packets are sent but won't be delivered.
steps: Show the NDR dashboard with no active alerts
Launch SCN-011 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA create and transmit the 10MB UDP payload (~15 seconds)
Switch to NDR console to show data exfiltration alert
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Deliver talking points while the UDP transfer is in progress — the CALDERA feed shows bytes sent in real time

scenarios.title: NDR: Web Application Attacks
scenarios.description: Sends 13 web application attack payloads via HTTP including Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), SQL Injection (SQLi), Remote Code Execution (RCE) injection, and cleartext credential leaks. Tests NDR detection of malicious HTTP payloads in transit.

expected_detections.detection: Flow Risk 1: XSS injection patterns in HTTP traffic
Flow Risk 2: SQL injection patterns in HTTP traffic
Flow Risk 3: RCE injection patterns in HTTP traffic
Flow Risk 26: Cleartext credentials in HTTP URL

universal: Web application attacks are the #1 initial access vector. NDR inspects HTTP traffic for attack patterns — XSS, SQLi, RCE injection — regardless of whether the target application is vulnerable.
NDR provides defense-in-depth: even if a WAF misses an attack, NDR detects the malicious payload pattern in network traffic.
financial_services: Online banking and payment portals are prime targets for SQLi and XSS. NDR detects attack attempts before they reach the application layer.
healthcare: Patient portals and EHR systems face constant web application attacks. NDR detects injection attempts across all web traffic.
manufacturing: Web-based SCADA and HMI interfaces are increasingly targeted. NDR monitors HTTP traffic to these systems for attack patterns.

se_runbook.setup: Ensure victim VM has curl.exe available (Windows 10+ includes it). NDR sensor must be monitoring the network segment.
steps: Show the NDR dashboard with no active alerts
Launch SCN-012 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA send 13 attack payloads (~30 seconds)
Switch to NDR console to show Flow Risk 1, 2, 3, and 26 alerts
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Deliver talking points after the attacks complete — show each Flow Risk category in the NDR console

scenarios.title: NDR: Bad User-Agent (Log4Shell)
scenarios.description: Sends an HTTP request with a Log4Shell-like JNDI User-Agent string. Tests NDR detection of suspicious User-Agent patterns commonly associated with Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) exploitation attempts.

expected_detections.detection: Flow Risk 11: Bad User-Agent header with JNDI lookup pattern

universal: Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) remains one of the most impactful vulnerabilities discovered. NDR can detect exploitation attempts by inspecting User-Agent headers for JNDI lookup patterns.
Even if endpoints are patched, NDR provides defense-in-depth by catching exploitation attempts at the network level before they reach the application.
financial_services: Financial institutions are high-value targets for Log4Shell due to the prevalence of Java-based middleware in banking systems.
healthcare: Healthcare systems often run legacy Java applications that may remain vulnerable to Log4Shell longer than other industries.
manufacturing: OT/ICS environments frequently use Java-based SCADA interfaces that could be vulnerable to JNDI injection attacks.

se_runbook.setup: Ensure victim VM has curl.exe available (Windows 10+ includes it). NDR sensor must be monitoring the network segment.
steps: Show the NDR dashboard with no active alerts
Launch SCN-013 from the SE Console
Wait for the CALDERA ability to complete (~5 seconds)
Switch to the NDR console and show the Flow Risk 11 alert
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Deliver talking points while waiting for the NDR alert to appear (typically 10-30 seconds after the request)

scenarios.title: NDR: Suspicious Exploit URL
scenarios.description: Accesses a URL with an exploit-like dot-prefixed path pattern commonly seen in exploit kits and vulnerability scanners. Tests NDR detection of suspicious web traffic patterns.

expected_detections.detection: Flow Risk 40: Possible exploit URL pattern detected

universal: Exploit kits and vulnerability scanners probe web servers using distinctive URL patterns. NDR detects these patterns in real time, alerting before an exploit succeeds.
This detection works regardless of the destination — NDR inspects the URL structure in transit, not the server response.
financial_services: Automated exploit scanners constantly probe financial web infrastructure. NDR catches these probes even when they target non-standard ports or paths.
healthcare: Medical device web interfaces are frequently scanned by exploit kits. NDR provides early warning of reconnaissance against these systems.
manufacturing: Web-exposed OT systems are targeted by specialized exploit scanners. NDR detects these probes before they find a vulnerability.

se_runbook.setup: Ensure victim VM has curl.exe available (Windows 10+ includes it). NDR sensor must be monitoring the network segment.
steps: Show the NDR dashboard with no active alerts
Launch SCN-014 from the SE Console
Wait for the CALDERA ability to complete (~5 seconds)
Switch to the NDR console and show the Flow Risk 40 alert
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Deliver talking points while waiting for the NDR alert to appear

scenarios.title: NDR: Network Port Scan
scenarios.description: Performs a TCP port scan across 10 hosts on the local subnet, probing 45 common service ports per host (450 total probes). Generates the network reconnaissance pattern that NDR sensors flag as port scanning activity.

expected_detections.detection: Horizontal port scan detected from single source
Network reconnaissance activity

universal: Port scanning is a foundational reconnaissance technique in nearly every attack chain. NDR detects the scanning pattern — rapid connection attempts across multiple hosts and ports — in real time.
Unlike firewall logs that only show blocked connections, NDR detects port scans across the entire network including east-west traffic between internal segments.
financial_services: Attackers scan financial networks for databases (3306, 5432, 1433), trading platforms, and management interfaces. NDR detects this reconnaissance before any exploitation occurs.
healthcare: Medical device discovery via port scanning is a precursor to many healthcare breaches. NDR alerts on scanning activity targeting device-heavy network segments.
manufacturing: Port scanning of OT networks can precede ICS-specific attacks. NDR monitors for reconnaissance activity crossing IT/OT boundaries.

se_runbook.setup: NDR sensor must be monitoring the network segment. The scan targets IPs .1 through .10 on the default subnet (192.168.1.0/24).
steps: Show the NDR dashboard with no active alerts
Launch SCN-015 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA scan 10 hosts across 45 ports (~60 seconds)
Switch to NDR console to show port scan / reconnaissance alert
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Deliver talking points while the scan is in progress — the live CALDERA feed shows discovered open ports

scenarios.title: Clear Windows Event Logs
scenarios.description: Clears the Windows Security, System, and Application event logs using the built-in wevtutil command. Simulates an attacker covering their tracks by destroying forensic evidence.

mitre_techniques.name: Indicator Removal: Clear Windows Event Logs

expected_detections.detection: Event log tampering — Security/System/Application logs cleared

universal: Event log clearing is one of the strongest indicators that an attacker was on the system. There is almost never a legitimate reason to clear the Security log on an endpoint.
Sophos detects log clearing in real time — the attacker can't erase evidence without generating an alert first.
financial_services: Regulatory requirements (PCI DSS, SOX) mandate log retention. An attacker clearing logs triggers both a security alert and a compliance violation.
healthcare: HIPAA requires audit log integrity. Sophos detects log tampering before the attacker can cover their tracks.
manufacturing: OT environments require forensic traceability. Endpoint detection catches log clearing even on systems without centralized logging.

se_runbook.setup: Victim VM must be running with Sophos agent. This test clears real event logs — they will be regenerated naturally over time.
steps: Show Sophos Central with no active alerts
Launch SCN-016 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA clear the three event logs (~5 seconds)
Show the Sophos alert for event log tampering
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Deliver talking points immediately after the alert appears

scenarios.title: Encoded PowerShell Execution
scenarios.description: Executes Base64-encoded PowerShell commands using the -EncodedCommand flag. Simulates attacker obfuscation — encoding commands to bypass simple string-based detection rules.

expected_detections.detection: Encoded/obfuscated PowerShell execution via -EncodedCommand

universal: Base64-encoded PowerShell is one of the top behavioral indicators of malicious activity. Legitimate scripts almost never use -EncodedCommand.
Sophos decodes and inspects PowerShell commands regardless of encoding — attackers can't hide behind Base64 obfuscation.
financial_services: Encoded PowerShell is the primary delivery mechanism for banking trojans and credential stealers targeting financial institutions.
healthcare: Healthcare ransomware groups frequently use encoded PowerShell for initial payload delivery and lateral movement.
manufacturing: Encoded PowerShell is used to bypass basic security controls on manufacturing systems that may only have signature-based protection.

se_runbook.setup: Victim VM must be running with Sophos agent.
steps: Show Sophos Central with no active alerts
Launch SCN-017 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA execute 3 encoded PowerShell commands (~10 seconds)
Show the Sophos alert for obfuscated script execution
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Point out the Base64 strings in the CALDERA feed, then show how Sophos decoded and flagged them

scenarios.title: LOLBin File Download (certutil)
scenarios.description: Uses certutil.exe — a legitimate Windows certificate utility — to download a file from the internet. This is the most common Living Off The Land (LOLBin) technique where attackers abuse built-in tools instead of deploying their own.

expected_detections.detection: Suspicious certutil usage — LOLBin file download

universal: Living Off The Land attacks use built-in Windows tools instead of deploying malware. certutil is the #1 abused LOLBin for file downloads.
Sophos monitors over 30 LOLBins for suspicious usage patterns — even though the tools are legitimate, their abuse is detectable by behavioral analysis.
financial_services: LOLBin attacks bypass allowlisting because certutil is a signed Microsoft binary. Behavioral detection is the only way to catch this.
healthcare: Healthcare environments often rely on allowlisting for security. LOLBin attacks bypass this control entirely — endpoint detection is essential.
manufacturing: Manufacturing systems often have limited security tooling. LOLBin detection catches attacks that use only built-in Windows capabilities.

se_runbook.setup: Victim VM must be running with Sophos agent and have internet access for the download.
steps: Show Sophos Central with no active alerts
Launch SCN-018 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA execute certutil download (~5 seconds)
Show the Sophos alert for LOLBin/certutil abuse
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Explain what LOLBins are while the download executes

scenarios.title: WMI Process Creation
scenarios.description: Uses Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to create and execute processes. WMI is a built-in Windows management framework that attackers abuse for execution and reconnaissance without deploying external tools.

expected_detections.detection: Suspicious WMI process creation activity

universal: WMI is present on every Windows system and can execute commands both locally and remotely. Attackers use it because it leaves minimal forensic artifacts compared to running commands directly.
Sophos monitors WMI for suspicious process creation chains — legitimate WMI usage has predictable patterns that differ from attacker behavior.
financial_services: WMI-based lateral movement is a key technique in APT attacks targeting financial institutions. Detecting WMI abuse blocks a critical attack path.
healthcare: WMI is used legitimately for device management in healthcare. Sophos distinguishes between normal WMI operations and attacker abuse.
manufacturing: WMI can reach systems across network segments. Detecting WMI abuse prevents attackers from pivoting from IT into OT environments.

se_runbook.setup: Victim VM must be running with Sophos agent.
steps: Show Sophos Central with no active alerts
Launch SCN-019 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA execute WMI commands (~10 seconds)
Show the Sophos alert for suspicious WMI activity
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Explain WMI's dual-use nature while commands execute

scenarios.title: Malicious Service Creation
scenarios.description: Creates a Windows service using sc.exe to simulate an attacker establishing persistence. The service is created with auto-start and a disguised display name, then cleaned up. Tests endpoint detection of unauthorized service installation.

mitre_techniques.name: Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service

expected_detections.detection: Unauthorized Windows service creation for persistence

universal: Service creation is one of the most reliable persistence mechanisms on Windows. Attackers create services with legitimate-sounding names to blend in with normal system services.
Sophos detects service creation by correlating the parent process, binary path, and timing — not just the service name.
financial_services: Backdoor services on financial systems enable persistent access for data theft. Detecting unauthorized service creation stops persistence before it takes hold.
healthcare: Persistent backdoors on healthcare systems can exfiltrate patient data over extended periods. Service creation detection is critical for HIPAA compliance.
manufacturing: Attackers targeting manufacturing often install persistent services on engineering workstations to maintain access to design files and OT network bridges.

se_runbook.setup: Victim VM must be running with Sophos agent.
steps: Show Sophos Central with no active alerts
Launch SCN-020 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA create and then remove the service (~5 seconds)
Show the Sophos alert for unauthorized service creation
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Explain how the service has an innocent-sounding name while the alert shows Sophos saw through the disguise

scenarios.title: Firewall: EICAR Test File Download
scenarios.description: Downloads the industry-standard EICAR anti-virus test file and PUA test file from Sophos's official test site (sophostest.com). The firewall IPS/AV engine should block the downloads before they reach the endpoint.

expected_detections.detection: Firewall IPS/AV blocks EICAR test file download
Firewall IPS/AV blocks PUA test file download

universal: The EICAR test file is the industry-standard test for anti-virus scanning. Every security product should detect it. The Sophos Firewall blocks it at the network level before it ever reaches the endpoint.
This demonstrates defense-in-depth: the firewall is the first line, and the endpoint agent provides a second layer if anything gets through.
financial_services: Financial regulators require layered security controls. Firewall-level malware blocking is a critical first layer for protecting banking infrastructure.
healthcare: HIPAA requires technical safeguards for ePHI. Firewall AV scanning prevents malware from reaching medical devices and clinical workstations.
manufacturing: Manufacturing environments often have unmanaged devices. Firewall-level AV scanning protects systems that can't run endpoint agents.

se_runbook.setup: Victim VM must have internet access through the Sophos Firewall. Firewall IPS/AV scanning must be enabled on the relevant firewall rule.
steps: Show the Sophos Firewall log viewer with no recent alerts
Launch SCN-021 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA attempt 3 downloads (~10 seconds)
Show the firewall IPS/AV block entries in the log viewer
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Explain EICAR and defense-in-depth while the downloads are attempted

scenarios.title: Firewall: IPS Signature Detection
scenarios.description: Accesses Sophos's official IPS test pages and downloads an IPS test PDF to trigger firewall Intrusion Prevention System signature detection.

expected_detections.detection: Firewall IPS detects test signature in HTTP traffic
Firewall IPS detects test signature in PDF download

universal: The IPS engine performs deep packet inspection, analyzing traffic content for known exploit patterns and attack signatures in real time.
Unlike a basic firewall that only checks ports and IPs, the Sophos Firewall IPS inspects the actual payload content for threats.
financial_services: IPS is a PCI DSS requirement for protecting cardholder data environments. Signature-based detection catches known exploits targeting financial applications.
healthcare: Medical device networks require IPS monitoring. The firewall inspects traffic to and from devices that can't run endpoint protection.
manufacturing: IPS protects OT networks from known exploits targeting industrial protocols and web interfaces on SCADA systems.

se_runbook.setup: Victim VM must have internet access through the Sophos Firewall. IPS must be enabled on the relevant firewall rule.
steps: Show the Sophos Firewall IPS log with no recent alerts
Launch SCN-022 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA access IPS test pages (~10 seconds)
Show the firewall IPS signature match alerts
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Explain deep packet inspection while the test runs

scenarios.title: Firewall: C2 Traffic Detection
scenarios.description: Accesses Sophos's C2 test landing page and simulates periodic beaconing callbacks. Tests firewall detection of Command & Control traffic patterns used by real malware.

expected_detections.detection: Firewall IPS detects C2 traffic signature
Firewall web filter blocks C2 category URL

universal: Command & Control is how attackers maintain access and send instructions to compromised systems. The firewall detects C2 traffic patterns even when the malware uses legitimate protocols like HTTP.
Beaconing detection identifies the regular interval callbacks that characterize C2 communication — a pattern that stands out from normal web browsing.
financial_services: APT groups targeting financial institutions rely on C2 channels for long-term persistent access. Firewall C2 detection disrupts these campaigns.
healthcare: Ransomware groups use C2 channels to stage attacks on healthcare systems. Blocking C2 at the firewall prevents the attack from progressing.
manufacturing: C2 detection at the firewall prevents attackers from communicating with compromised systems in the OT network.

se_runbook.setup: Victim VM must have internet access through the Sophos Firewall. IPS and web filtering must be enabled.
steps: Show the Sophos Firewall log viewer
Launch SCN-023 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA access C2 page then beacon 5 times (~30 seconds)
Show the firewall C2 detection and web filter alerts
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Point out the regular beaconing interval in the CALDERA feed while explaining C2 patterns

scenarios.title: Firewall: Phishing Site Block
scenarios.description: Attempts to access URLs categorized as phishing, fraud, and criminal activity by Sophos web filtering. The firewall should block access and serve a block page.

expected_detections.detection: Firewall web filter blocks phishing/fraud category URL
Firewall web filter blocks criminal activity category URL

universal: Phishing is the #1 initial access vector in breaches. The Sophos Firewall blocks known phishing sites using SophosLabs threat intelligence before the user can interact with the page.
URL categorization is updated continuously from SophosLabs — new phishing sites are categorized within minutes of discovery.
financial_services: Banking phishing is the most common form of financial fraud. Firewall web filtering blocks phishing sites targeting banking credentials.
healthcare: Healthcare credential phishing leads to unauthorized access to patient records. Web filtering prevents staff from reaching phishing pages.
manufacturing: Spear phishing targeting engineering and procurement staff is common in manufacturing. Web filtering blocks these attacks at the network level.

se_runbook.setup: Victim VM must have internet access through the Sophos Firewall. Web filtering must be enabled with phishing category set to block.
steps: Show the Sophos Firewall web filter log
Launch SCN-024 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA attempt to access phishing URLs (~10 seconds)
Show the firewall block entries and explain URL categorization
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Explain SophosLabs URL categorization while the blocks are displayed

scenarios.title: Firewall: Malware Site Block
scenarios.description: Attempts to access URLs across 5 malicious categories: spyware/malware, C2, hacking resources, spam, and anonymizers. Tests firewall web filtering across multiple threat categories.

expected_detections.detection: Firewall web filter blocks spyware/malware category
Firewall web filter blocks C2 category
Firewall web filter blocks hacking resources category
Firewall web filter blocks spam/anonymizer categories

universal: Web filtering is more than just blocking inappropriate content — it's a critical security layer that prevents access to known malicious infrastructure.
The Sophos Firewall categorizes millions of URLs across threat categories. This test shows blocking across 5 different threat types in seconds.
financial_services: Comprehensive web filtering is a regulatory requirement for financial institutions under PCI DSS and SOX compliance.
healthcare: Web filtering prevents healthcare staff from accidentally accessing malware distribution sites that could compromise clinical systems.
manufacturing: Blocking access to hacking resources and anonymizers prevents insider threats and unauthorized tools on manufacturing networks.

se_runbook.setup: Victim VM must have internet access through the Sophos Firewall. Web filtering must be enabled with security categories set to block.
steps: Show the Sophos Firewall web filter log
Launch SCN-025 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA attempt to access 5 malicious category URLs (~15 seconds)
Show the firewall block entries across all 5 categories
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Walk through each category as it appears in the firewall log

scenarios.title: Firewall: DNS Tunneling
scenarios.description: Simulates data exfiltration via DNS by encoding sensitive data (credentials, keys, configs) into DNS subdomain queries. Sends 20 DNS lookups with Base64-encoded data to test firewall DNS inspection.

expected_detections.detection: Firewall DNS inspection flags anomalous subdomain query pattern
Firewall detects DNS tunneling or DNS exfiltration

universal: DNS tunneling is one of the stealthiest exfiltration techniques because DNS traffic is rarely blocked and often not inspected. Attackers encode stolen data into DNS queries that pass through most firewalls.
Sophos Firewall DNS inspection detects anomalous query patterns — unusually long subdomains, high query frequency, and Base64-like encoding in domain names.
financial_services: DNS tunneling has been used to exfiltrate credit card data from POS systems. Firewall DNS inspection catches this exfiltration channel.
healthcare: Patient data exfiltrated via DNS tunneling bypasses traditional DLP controls. Firewall DNS inspection provides coverage for this blind spot.
manufacturing: Trade secrets and proprietary designs can be exfiltrated via DNS. DNS inspection at the firewall detects this covert channel.

se_runbook.setup: Victim VM must use the Sophos Firewall as its DNS resolver. DNS inspection must be enabled.
steps: Show the Sophos Firewall DNS log
Launch SCN-026 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA send 20 encoded DNS queries (~15 seconds)
Show the firewall DNS inspection alerts for anomalous queries
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Explain how DNS tunneling works while queries are being sent — point out the encoded subdomain labels in the CALDERA feed

scenarios.title: Firewall: Policy Violation (Outbound SSH/RDP)
scenarios.description: Attempts outbound SSH and RDP connections to 5 external IP addresses. Tests firewall egress policy enforcement — management protocols should be blocked from leaving the network.

expected_detections.detection: Firewall blocks outbound SSH connections (port 22)
Firewall blocks outbound RDP connections (port 3389)

universal: Egress filtering is a security best practice that most organizations neglect. Blocking outbound SSH and RDP prevents attackers from establishing reverse tunnels after compromising an endpoint.
The Sophos Firewall enforces protocol-level egress policies — not just port blocking, but application-aware filtering that catches tunneled protocols.
financial_services: PCI DSS requires egress filtering for cardholder data environments. Blocking outbound management protocols prevents data exfiltration via reverse shells.
healthcare: Outbound RDP from clinical workstations is a strong indicator of compromise. Firewall policy enforcement catches this immediately.
manufacturing: Blocking outbound SSH/RDP from the manufacturing network prevents attackers from establishing persistent remote access to OT systems.

se_runbook.setup: Sophos Firewall must have egress policies blocking outbound SSH (22) and RDP (3389). Target IPs use RFC 5737 documentation ranges (safe).
steps: Show the Sophos Firewall policy rules
Launch SCN-027 from the SE Console
Watch CALDERA attempt 10 outbound connections (~15 seconds)
Show the firewall policy violation log entries
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Explain egress filtering while the connection attempts are blocked

scenarios.title: Live Attack Chain: Credential Theft to Ransomware
scenarios.description: Full four-phase kill chain designed for on-stage demos. Visible attacker terminals open on the victim desktop at each phase. Recon → LSASS credential theft (Sophos detection trigger) → Scheduled task persistence → Ransomware artifacts on the Desktop. On the unmanaged VM the full chain completes visibly. On the Sophos-protected VM, Intercept X blocks at Phase 2 with a live alert.

expected_detections.detection: LSASS memory access by non-system process (comsvcs.dll MiniDump)
Suspicious scheduled task registration at logon (WindowsUpdateHelper)
Ransom note file creation on user Desktop
Mass .encrypted extension file creation

universal: This is the exact kill chain we see in real MDR cases — recon, credential theft, persistence, ransomware
Notice the unmanaged VM: the attack completes in full, step by step, completely visible
On the Sophos-protected VM, Intercept X stopped it at Phase 2 — the attacker never reached ransomware
One blocked LSASS dump prevented the entire attack chain from completing
financial_services: Stolen credentials from LSASS give the attacker access to every system those credentials can reach — trading systems, core banking, payment rails
The scheduled task means the attacker survives a reboot and re-establishes access even after an incident response
healthcare: In a healthcare breach, Phase 2 is where clinical systems become reachable — one credential dump gives access to EHR, PACS, and clinical workstations
Ransomware on clinical systems is a patient safety event, not just an IT event
manufacturing: The persistence mechanism in Phase 3 is how attackers bridge from IT to OT — a scheduled task on an IT workstation can reach engineering systems
We've seen this exact chain in manufacturing incidents targeting SCADA and PLC systems

se_runbook.setup: Run s.ps1 on both victim VMs before the demo. Both VMs should have sandcat agent running. Verify Sophos is active on the protected VM. Both VMs should show their custom wallpaper. Open Stage Dive from the SE Console nav.
steps: Open Stage Dive — navigate to Slide 2 (unmanaged victim) and Slide 3 (split view)
Launch SCN-028 from the SE Console
Slide 2: watch the attacker terminal open on the unmanaged victim — narrate Phase 1 recon
Phase 2: LSASS dump runs — on unmanaged it succeeds, credential_dump_complete.txt appears on Desktop
Switch to Slide 3 (split view): Sophos alert fires on the protected VM at Phase 2
Continue narrating as Phases 3 and 4 complete on the unmanaged VM
Phase 4: ransom note + ENCRYPTED_FILES folder appear on the unmanaged Desktop — the close
se_runbook.talking_points_timing: Deliver talking points during Phase 2 while Sophos alert is visible — maximum impact
